title: Add Volumes of Rectangular Prisms
description: Find the total volume of a composite solid by adding the volumes of its rectangular prism parts.
condition: A solid is decomposed into two or more rectangular prisms with known or computable volumes.
response: Add the individual prism volumes to obtain the total volume.
label: Principle / Model
example: If one prism has volume $6 \times 2 \times 1 = 12$ and another has $3 \times 2 \times 1 = 6$, then total volume $= 12 + 6 = 18$ cubic units.

title: Compare Volume to Known Capacity
description: Compare the volume of a partially filled prism to a known benchmark number of cubes that could fit inside.
condition: A rectangular prism is partially filled with unit cubes and a benchmark capacity (e.g., 12 cubes) is implied or shown.
response: Determine whether the total volume is less than, equal to, or greater than the benchmark.
label: Principle / Model
example: If 12 cubes fit inside but do not fill the prism completely, then the volume is greater than $12$ cubic units.

title: Compare Volumes by Counting Cubes
description: Determine which solid has greater volume by comparing the total number of unit cubes in each figure.
condition: Two or more solids built from unit cubes with known or countable totals.
response: Identify the solid with the greater number of unit cubes as having greater volume.
label: Principle / Model
example: If Figure A has 10 unit cubes and Figure B has 9 unit cubes, then since $10 > 9$, Figure A has greater volume.

title: Compute Area of Rectangular Base
description: Find the area of a rectangular base by multiplying its length and width.
condition: Given the length and width of a rectangle.
response: Multiply length × width to find the area.
label: Principle / Model
example: If a base measures 7 in by 4 in, then its area is $7 \times 4 = 28$ square inches.
notes: Area formula for rectangles derived from array structure.

title: Compute Volume Using Layers
description: Find the volume of a rectangular prism by multiplying the number of cubes in one layer (base) by the number of layers (height).
condition: A rectangular prism structured in equal layers with a known number of cubes per layer and a known number of layers.
response: Multiply cubes per layer by number of layers to find total volume.
label: Principle / Model
example: If one layer has 15 cubes and there are 2 layers, then the volume is $15 \times 2 = 30$ cubic units.

title: Count Unit Cubes in a Figure
description: Determine the total volume of a solid by counting all visible and hidden unit cubes that compose it.
condition: A solid composed of unit cubes is represented visually.
response: Count each unit cube once to determine the total number of cubes.
label: Production / Skill
example: A prism shows 3 cubes across, 5 cubes deep, and 2 cubes high. Counting all cubes gives $3 \times 5 \times 2 = 30$ cubes.
notes: Counting may be done individually or via structured grouping.

title: Decompose Composite Solid into Rectangular Prisms
description: Recognize that a composite 3D figure can be partitioned into two or more rectangular prisms for easier volume calculation.
condition: Given a 3D composite figure composed of rectangular prism parts.
response: Partition the figure into non-overlapping rectangular prisms.
label: Principle
example: An L-shaped solid can be split into two rectangular prisms, each with its own length, width, and height.

title: Evaluate Multiplication Expression
description: Compute the numerical value of a multiplication expression.
condition: Given a numerical multiplication expression (two or more factors).
label: Production / Skill

title: Interpret Product as Layers of Cubes
description: Interpret a multiplication expression as representing layers of equal numbers of unit cubes in a prism.
condition: A multiplication expression represents (number of layers) × (cubes per layer).
response: Recognize the product as the total number of unit cubes (volume).
label: Concept
example: $5 \times 12$ can represent 5 layers of 12 cubes each, for a total of 60 cubes.
notes: Response classified as Constant because it is the same interpretation pattern (total cubes) across cases.???

title: Interpret Volume as Base Area × Height
description: Explain volume as the number of layers of equal base area multiplied by the height.
condition: Given a rectangular prism with known base area and height (or an expression like a × b representing layers).
response: State that volume equals base area × height and interpret one factor as number of layers.
label: Principle / Model
example: If the base area is $8 \times 6 = 48$ square units and the height is 4 units, then volume is $4 \times 48 = 192$ cubic units.
notes: Connects area concept to layered structure of prisms.

title: Multiply Whole Numbers
description: Compute the product of two whole numbers to determine total quantity.
condition: Two whole numbers to be multiplied.
label: Principle / Model
notes: Basic multiplication fact fluency supports volume calculation. [merged: condition varies] [merged: response varies] [merged: label varies]

title: Recognize Equivalent Volume Expressions
description: Identify different numerical expressions that represent the same volume of a rectangular prism.
condition: Multiple expressions are given that may represent the same rectangular prism volume.
response: Select all expressions whose value and structure correspond to the prism’s volume.
label: Principle / Model
example: For a prism with dimensions $3, 4, 5$, expressions such as $3 \times 4 \times 5$, $20 + 20 + 20$, and $5 \times 12$ all equal $60$ and represent its volume.

title: Track and State Cubic Units
description: Determine and state the correct cubic units for volume based on the linear units provided.
condition: Side lengths or base area and height are given with linear units (e.g., inches, feet).
response: Express the volume in corresponding cubic units (e.g., cubic inches, cubic feet).
label: Rule / Plan
example: If dimensions are in feet, such as $6 \text{ ft} \times 2 \text{ ft} \times 1 \text{ ft}$, the volume is expressed in cubic feet.
notes: Rationale marked No because unit conventions are defined rather than derived.???

title: Volume as Length × Width × Height
description: Compute the volume of a rectangular prism by multiplying its length, width, and height.
condition: Given the length, width, and height of a rectangular prism.
label: Principle / Model
example: If $l=2$, $w=5$, and $h=7$, then volume $= 2 \times 5 \times 7 = 70$ cubic units.
